name: VEGAN DRIED FISH AND BITTER MELON SALAD
intro: In gỏi khổ qua cá chay, dried bean curd replaces the dried fish. You can use karela (Indian bitter melon) or Chinese bitter melon for this recipe. Karela has a darker-green skin and tighter, more pronounced bumps while the Chinese melon is lighter green and has wider, shallower bumps. To help reduce the bitterness of the melon, see step 4.

ingredients.item: large or 2 small karela or Chinese bitter melons, seeds removed and thinly sliced
round leaf mint or mint, roughly chopped if leaves are large

method: In a medium bowl, mash the chao cubes with a spoon until it’s a smooth paste. Add sugar and stock powder, and mix well. Add flour and stir briskly until you get a thick, smooth, homogenous mixture.
Spread a thin layer of the mixture on each dried bean curd stick.

Fill the bottom of a saucepan with about 2 inches (5 cm) of oil and place over medium heat. Check with a cooking thermometer and when oil reaches 320°F (160°C), add dried bean curd sticks and deep-fry until crisp and golden brown (1 to 2 minutes). Remove fried bean curd sticks, drain excess oil, and then cut into small pieces (about 2-inches/5-cm long).
Scrape all seeds and white spongy part off melons, slice them thinly crosswise, and then rinse a few times. (Optional: To reduce the bitterness, you can blanch the slices, and then drain.)
Toss bitter melons, carrots, and salt in a large bowl, and let sit for 15 minutes.
Rinse, squeeze out excess water, and then place in a large bowl.
In a small bowl, combine soy sauce, sugar, and lime juice, and stir well until sugar is dissolved.
Add the dressing to the bowl with the salad mixture and toss well.
Add mint, cilantro, dried fish, pickled scallion heads (if using), and roasted peanuts, and mix together.
Serve with sesame rice crackers (if using).
